annotations.text: The most compelling evidence pointing away from pure atherosclerosis is the multifocal, irregular nature of the vertebral artery stenoses, particularly their asymmetry and distribution along the dural ring and mid-vertebral segments—regions less commonly affected by typical atherosclerotic plaques, which tend to localize at ostial or bifurcation sites.

The presence of non-calcified, circumferential stenoses at the origin of the left vertebral artery, coupled with elevated intracranial flow velocities on transcranial Doppler and the absence of significant internal carotid disease, suggests a process that is not simply age-related plaque accumulation.
ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV), particularly microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), must be considered as a leading diagnosis.
The dramatic rise in C-reactive protein (29.8 mg/L) strongly implies systemic inflammation, and the positive ANCA serology—though preliminary—is perhaps the most significant clue.
The absence of alveolar hemorrhage, glomerulonephritis, or pulmonary nodules does not exclude MPA; up to 30% of patients present with isolated neurological manifestations.
The borderline lupus anticoagulant and elevated anticardiolipin antibodies (IgG 56.8 GPL, IgM 28.5 MPL) suggest a secondary antiphospholipid syndrome (APS), which could coexist with vasculitis.
According to the Sydney criteria, this meets the serologic threshold for antiphospholipid syndrome (APS).
The challenge lies in distinguishing between atherosclerotic progression, a primary vasculitis, a prothrombotic state, and a systemic connective tissue disorder with vascular involvement.
The patient’s response to therapy also informs the differential.
The coexistence of APS with an underlying autoimmune vasculitis (secondary APS) is well documented, particularly in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) or MPA.

This case underscores the importance of considering vasculitis in any elderly patient with recurrent, multifocal strokes in the posterior circulation, especially when conventional risk factors and therapies fail to explain the clinical trajectory.
